content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 7, 2018

Tricia Hynes
Deputy City Attorney
One Frank H. Ogawa Plaza, 6th Floor
Oakland, CA 94612

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-18-133

Dear Ms. Hynes:

        This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the Political Reform Act and
Government Code Section 1090, et seq.¹ Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law.
We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and
any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

        In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Alameda County
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that,
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

        If City Councilmembers vote on a contract that includes language allocating tickets to "the
City of Oakland" that are subsequently distributed to Councilmembers pursuant to the City's ticket
policy, would the Council be in violation of Government Code section 1090?

CONCLUSION

        No. With respect to Section 1090, the Councilmembers do not have financial interests in the
contract at issue under these facts.

_______________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-133
Page No. 2

FACTS

The Oakland Government Ethics Act ("GEA") was passed in 2014 to establish local ethics laws that mirror and supplement the California Political Reform Act (the "Act"), among other state laws.² The GEA incorporates by reference all gift requirements and restrictions in the PRA and imposes additional, lower gift limits on Oakland public officials. The City's Ethics Commission is charged with ensuring compliance with the GEA through enforcement, education and advice, and policy development. As part of its duties, the City's Ethics Commission provides advice and educational materials to public servants.

In 2009 the Oakland City Council adopted a revised City of Oakland Policy for Receipt and Distribution of Passes and Tickets ("Ticket Policy") to satisfy the requirements of Regulation 18944.1. The Ticket Policy further provides the procedures for distribution of tickets to and from the City ("City tickets") and its officials, "by which those tickets or passes will not be considered gifts under the Political Reform Act."³ These procedures include a list of "governmental purposes" for the distribution of City tickets, restrictions on the transfer of City Tickets directly from City officials to third parties, and a requirement for the receiving official to submit electronic Form 802 information within a certain time period for subsequent approval by the ticket distributing official.

The City receives monthly batches of luxury suite tickets to all sporting, recreational, and entertainment events at the Oakland Coliseum and Oracle Arena pursuant to a contract for the use of City property. Tickets are delivered to the Secretary of the City Council who then makes two tickets available to City Councilmembers for their personal use.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal. App. 4th 289, 333.) Nonetheless, an official has a financial interest in a contract only when there is a sufficient connection between the contract in question and interest held by the official.

The potential contract you have identified involves luxury suite tickets to all sporting, recreational, and entertainment events at the Oakland Coliseum and Oracle Arena pursuant to a contract for the use of City property. City Councilmembers would vote to approve this contract, which would include a provision allocating tickets to the City.

Regulation 18944.1 addresses the receipt and distribution of tickets and passes by a public agency. It provides a method whereby the agency may establish a procedure under which the tickets

² Oakland Municipal Code Chapter 2.25.

³ City of Oakland Policy for Receipt and Distribution of Passes and Tickets, Resolution 82032, adopted May 19, 2009.

File No. A-18-133
Page No. 3

or passes will not be considered "gifts" for purposes of the Act. Nothing in Regulation 18944.1
requires a state or local agency to follow its provisions. It merely provides an optional procedure for
a public agency to use as a method for distributing certain types of tickets or passes to events where
the tickets or passes, distributed to accomplish a public purpose, will be exempt from the Act's
definition of "gift." The distribution of tickets or passes by the agency must be made in accordance
with a policy adopted by the agency in order for the tickets or passes to be exempt for the Act's
definition of "gift," and Regulation 18944.1(d)(1)(C) sets forth the requirements for the agency's
written ticket distribution policy.

The contract includes a provision allocating tickets to the City of Oakland. Theses tickets
are made available to council members under the terms of a Ticket Policy adopted pursuant the
requirements of Regulation 18944.1. Since the ticket or pass is in consideration for the official's
services to the agency, it would be considered government "salary" (Section 82030(b)(2) and not
subject to reporting on the official's Statement of Economic Interests. Under these facts, the
officials would not have an interest in the tickets under the Act.

Under these circumstances, officials do not have a reportable interest in the tickets under the
Act or under Section 1090. In making this determination, we are guided by Lexin v. Superior Court
(2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1090-91, which applies the "in pari materia" canon of statutory
construction in determining that Section 1090 should be harmonized with the Act when possible. As
explained by the court, "it is well established that Section 1090 and the Act are 'in pari materia.'"
(Ibid.). Based on the facts provided, we conclude that Section 1090 does not apply in this context.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:jgl

sections.question:
If City Councilmembers vote on a contract that includes language allocating tickets to "the 
City of Oakland" that are subsequently distributed to Councilmembers pursuant to the City's ticket 
policy, would the Council be in violation of Government Code section 1090?
sections.conclusion:
No. With respect to Section 1090, the Councilmembers do not have financial interests in the 
contract at issue under these facts.

tickets available to City Councilmembers for their personal use.

City of Oakland" that are subsequently distributed to Councilmembers pursuant to the City's ticket 
policy, would the Council be in violation of Government Code section 1090?

CONCLUSION: No. With respect to Section 1090, the Councilmembers do not have financial interests in the 
contract at issue under these facts.
